topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in Clara’s inbox with the brutal finality of a dropped stone. She stared at the screen, the cheerful, brightly colored invitation mocking her with its cheerful font. *Event Cancelled.* A slow, cold dread began to coil in her stomach, tightening with every second that passed. She reread the message, the words blurring slightly, before her eyes snagged on the line stating the reason. It was vague, bureaucratic, utterly devoid of warmth. Her hands, resting on the cool wooden desk, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. She pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, a sudden, sharp noise in the quiet apartment. A breath hitched in her throat, shallow and quick. She needed to check her phone, to call someone, but her fingers felt clumsy, unresponsive. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in her chest.

[story 2]

Mark kept glancing at the calendar on the wall, the circled date now seeming like a cruel joke. He picked up his coffee mug, the ceramic warm against his palms, but the heat did little to soothe the sudden chill that had settled deep in his bones. He clicked on the group chat, his thumb hovering over the notification. There. A single, curt message confirming the cancellation. The noise of the city outside—the distant sirens, the rumble of traffic—seemed suddenly too loud, too intrusive. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of his living room three times before stopping dead in front of the window. He pressed his forehead against the cool glass, trying to steady the rapid pulse hammering beneath his skin. He felt a strange pressure building behind his eyes, a heavy weight that made focusing on anything else impossible.

[story 3]

When Sarah read the notification, her entire body seemed to freeze mid-movement. The vibrant anticipation that had been bubbling inside her evaporated instantly, replaced by a suffocating vacuum. She let the phone slip from her grasp; it hit the rug with a soft thud that sounded deafening in the sudden stillness. Her jaw locked so tightly she could feel the strain in her molars. She walked to the kitchen counter, gripping the edge until her knuckles were white, staring blankly at the fruit bowl. Every small sound—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant neighbor’s music—was magnified, demanding attention. A wave of nausea washed over her, making her lean heavily against the counter, fighting the urge to simply sink to the floor.
